story:
The summer blues had set in. School had been out two weeks, with two long months to go, and guilt shadowed every hour that was supposed to be relaxing and connected. She fretted about her oldest, seven and already weary of school. He had cried some mornings before he even got out of bed. He could read almost anything, which made her proud, but at what cost? The academics were intense from kindergarten on. No pizza parties, carnivals, Halloween parade, or field trips—maybe a pajama day if they were lucky. She wondered if they still brought out that big parachute in PE.

She dreamed of giving him a magical summer, the kind she’d had before time and screens took over. The pressure mounted: you only get 18 summers. She wanted to be a happy, present mom, not one frayed by noise and need. But it was only 9 a.m., they’d been up for three hours, and she had already lost the snack-versus-meal battle with a toddler who said she wasn’t hungry when it was time to sit. She traded fruit snacks for bites of real food. She clicked off a TV no one was watching and got offended looks anyway.

It wasn’t that she didn’t try. She set up a paddling pool, water table, a blow-up bouncy castle—only for them to lose interest before she’d finished filling and inflating. If she didn’t organize a game, they just wandered. “You guys figure out a game,” she urged.

She took him swimming the day before, let him ride on her back and splash her hair snarled to knots because it made him laugh. Earlier in the week, she’d paid twenty-four dollars for an indoor playground with stamped hands and overpriced snacks. But he needed other seven-year-old boys, not a middle-aged mom with a half-torn heart. He missed his friends. She missed his friends.

Even screens failed. The iPad lay abandoned. Because he rarely had it, maybe it wasn’t hypnotic; he wanted her right beside him, narrating. She tried to care about a block village he hadn’t even built, but he grew impatient with her questions and her lack of game knowledge.

He invented a new game: pretending to take his sister’s toys. He never touched them, but it still set off the squealing mine, mine, mine. “Stop, please,” she said. “She doesn’t understand you’re joking. Help me pick up if you’re bored.” Tiles, train tracks, dollhouse bits littered the floor. She swept crushed goldfish and glared at a sticky Capri Sun straw sleeve glued to the hardwood. When she jostled the table and her forgotten coffee leapt to the floor, her sanity seemed to follow.

She sneaked a snack. The crinkled wrappers felt like evidence. A Twinkie wouldn’t fix months of tattling, teasing, messes. “I need a minute!” she called, locking her bedroom door. She gripped the mattress, breathed in, held five seconds, blew out through clenched teeth. Therapy had given her that.

She thought of her own mother and those open-door summers: in or out? Out, always. A pack of neighborhood kids, Super Soakers, Slip ’N Slides, a tree with boards nailed into it and a tire swing. Impromptu games—flag football, kick the can, red light/green light, cops and robbers—and they policed themselves. They biked to 7-Eleven for snacks, to McDonald’s for cheeseburgers and 50-cent cones, to the video store for a movie. They even shot cap guns at cars at the end of the street. No one worried. And that was the problem. Neglect was heavy. The news told of kids who vanished: a little girl taken at the ice cream truck, her pink bike’s wheels still spinning. There was no sunscreen, too many unattended injuries, and once a fourteen-year-old had tried twice to lure her when she was eight. She kicked him and told no one. What if she hadn’t? Freedom had its cost.

She wished she could give her kids one good summer day without the risk.

The metal doorknob rattled. Four minutes gone. She pinched the bridge of her nose, breathed again, and opened the door. He stood there, new teeth too big for his face. “What are we doing today?” he asked. “You want to give us one good summer day without the risk, right?”

She exhaled into a tight smile. “I haven’t decided yet. Help me come up with a plan.”
